milestone_description: Make the performance, reliability, and quality of RASS fully visible and provable. This phase introduces distributed tracing, a metrics dashboard, an automated evaluation discipline, and a compelling performance story backed by real numbers. Engineers should be able to identify bottlenecks, prove retrieval quality, and demonstrate system health at a glance.

issues.title: [Phase E] Add distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry across all services
issues.body:
## Problem

RASS is a multi-service system (frontend, mcp-server, embedding-service, rass-engine-service, OpenSearch, Redis, Postgres) but has no distributed tracing. When a query takes 3 seconds, it is impossible to determine which service or which operation is responsible. There is no way to:
- Trace a request from the frontend through mcp-server → rass-engine-service → OpenSearch
- Identify which stage of ingestion is slow (parse, embed, or index)
- Correlate logs from different services for a single user request

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Add OpenTelemetry SDK to each Node.js service
```bash
npm install @opentelemetry/sdk-node @opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node \
  @opentelemetry/exporter-otlp-grpc
```

### 2. Create an `otel.js` bootstrap file in each service
```js
// src/otel.js — imported as the FIRST thing in the service entry point
import { NodeSDK } from '@opentelemetry/sdk-node';
import { OTLPTraceExporter } from '@opentelemetry/exporter-otlp-grpc';
import { getNodeAutoInstrumentations } from '@opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node';

const sdk = new NodeSDK({
  traceExporter: new OTLPTraceExporter({ url: process.env.OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT }),
  instrumentations: [getNodeAutoInstrumentations()],
});
sdk.start();
```

### 3. Add custom spans for key operations
- `retrieval.embedQuery` — query embedding call
- `retrieval.hybridSearch` — OpenSearch KNN + keyword query
- `retrieval.rerank` — cross-encoder reranking
- `ingestion.parse` — document parsing
- `ingestion.chunk` — chunking
- `ingestion.embed` — batch embedding call
- `ingestion.index` — OpenSearch bulk index

### 4. Add Jaeger to docker-compose
```yaml
jaeger:
  image: jaegertracing/all-in-one:1.53
  ports:
    - "16686:16686"  # Jaeger UI
    - "4317:4317"    # OTLP gRPC
  environment:
    - COLLECTOR_OTLP_ENABLED=true
```

### 5. Propagate trace context between services
- Ensure HTTP headers (`traceparent`, `tracestate`) are forwarded by mcp-server when proxying to rass-engine and embedding services
- Auto-instrumentation handles this for `express` and `axios`; verify it works end-to-end

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A complete trace from frontend upload to OpenSearch indexing is visible in Jaeger UI
- [ ] A complete trace from query submission to SSE stream completion is visible in Jaeger UI
- [ ] All services export traces to Jaeger via OTLP gRPC
- [ ] Custom spans for the six named operations appear with correct durations
- [ ] Trace context is propagated across all three Node.js services (no broken traces)
- [ ] Jaeger UI is accessible at `http://localhost:16686` in the development Docker Compose stack
- [ ] Adding tracing does not increase end-to-end query latency by more than 5 ms (measured)

issues.title: [Phase E] Implement a metrics dashboard with Prometheus and Grafana
issues.body:
## Problem

RASS has no runtime metrics. There is no way to monitor:
- Request throughput and error rates per service
- Query latency distribution (p50, p95, p99)
- Ingestion queue depth and worker throughput
- OpenSearch query latency and error rate
- LLM API latency and token usage
- System resource utilization (CPU, memory, disk)

Without metrics, capacity planning, SLA monitoring, and incident response are all blind.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Add `prom-client` to each Node.js service
```bash
npm install prom-client
```

### 2. Define and instrument key metrics
```js
// Counters
const requestsTotal = new Counter({ name: 'rass_requests_total', labelNames: ['service', 'route', 'status'] });
const llmApiErrors = new Counter({ name: 'rass_llm_api_errors_total', labelNames: ['provider'] });

// Histograms
const queryLatency = new Histogram({ name: 'rass_query_latency_seconds', labelNames: ['stage'], buckets: [0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5] });
const ingestionDuration = new Histogram({ name: 'rass_ingestion_duration_seconds', labelNames: ['stage'] });

// Gauges
const queueDepth = new Gauge({ name: 'rass_ingestion_queue_depth', labelNames: ['queue'] });
const activeWorkers = new Gauge({ name: 'rass_ingestion_active_workers' });
```

### 3. Expose `/metrics` endpoint
- Add `GET /metrics` to each service that returns Prometheus text format
- Secure with `Authorization: Bearer <METRICS_TOKEN>` to prevent public exposure

### 4. Add Prometheus and Grafana to docker-compose
```yaml
prometheus:
  image: prom/prometheus:v2.50.1
  volumes:
    - ./monitoring/prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml
grafana:
  image: grafana/grafana:10.3.1
  ports:
    - "3000:3000"
  volumes:
    - ./monitoring/grafana/dashboards:/var/lib/grafana/dashboards
```

### 5. Create pre-built Grafana dashboards
Create JSON dashboard definitions in `monitoring/grafana/dashboards/`:
- `rass-overview.json` — request rate, error rate, latency percentiles
- `rass-ingestion.json` — queue depth, worker throughput, stage durations
- `rass-retrieval.json` — search latency, rerank latency, hit rate

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All three Node.js services expose `/metrics` in Prometheus format
- [ ] Prometheus scrapes all services; `rass_requests_total` metric is visible in Prometheus UI
- [ ] Grafana dashboard `rass-overview.json` loads without errors and shows live data
- [ ] `rass_query_latency_seconds` histogram shows p50/p95/p99 breakdowns per pipeline stage
- [ ] `rass_ingestion_queue_depth` gauge reflects the actual BullMQ queue depth in real time
- [ ] Grafana and Prometheus are accessible in the development Docker Compose stack without additional setup
- [ ] Dashboard JSON files are committed and loaded automatically via Grafana provisioning

issues.title: [Phase E] Create an end-to-end performance benchmarking suite
issues.body:
## Problem

There are no documented performance baselines for RASS. The following questions are currently unanswerable:
- What is the maximum throughput (queries per second) the system can sustain?
- How does ingestion throughput scale with document size?
- What is the p99 query latency under concurrent load?
- Which component is the bottleneck at scale (embedding, OpenSearch, LLM)?

Without benchmarks, performance claims are unverifiable and regressions are invisible.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Create a load testing harness
- Use `k6` (Go-based, scriptable) for HTTP load testing
- Create `benchmarks/query_load.js` — ramps from 1 to 50 concurrent users, measures latency and error rate for the `/stream-ask` endpoint
- Create `benchmarks/ingestion_load.js` — uploads 10 sample documents concurrently, measures queue depth and completion time

### 2. Define benchmark scenarios
```js
// query_load.js scenarios
export const options = {
  scenarios: {
    steady_state: { executor: 'constant-vus', vus: 10, duration: '2m' },
    ramp_up:      { executor: 'ramping-vus', stages: [{ target: 50, duration: '5m' }] },
  },
  thresholds: {
    http_req_duration: ['p(95)<3000'],  // 95th percentile < 3 s
    http_req_failed:   ['rate<0.01'],   // error rate < 1 %
  },
};
```

### 3. Add benchmark runner script
Create `benchmarks/run_benchmarks.sh`:
- Starts the Docker Compose stack
- Ingests the benchmark corpus (pre-seeded sample documents)
- Runs each k6 scenario and saves results to `benchmarks/results/<timestamp>/`
- Generates a Markdown summary table

### 4. Establish and document baseline
- Run the suite on a reference machine (documented specs)
- Commit baseline results to `benchmarks/baselines/`
- Add comparison logic: fail if any p95 latency regresses by > 20 %

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `benchmarks/query_load.js` runs against a local RASS stack without manual configuration
- [ ] All defined `thresholds` pass on the reference hardware (p95 < 3 s, error rate < 1 %)
- [ ] `benchmarks/ingestion_load.js` measures and reports ingestion throughput (MB/s and docs/min)
- [ ] Benchmark results are saved in a parseable JSON format for comparison
- [ ] A documented baseline is committed to `benchmarks/baselines/baseline_v1.json`
- [ ] `benchmarks/run_benchmarks.sh` exits non-zero if any threshold is breached

issues.title: [Phase E] Implement structured logging with correlation IDs across all services
issues.body:
## Problem

The current services use `console.log` / `console.error` for logging. This produces:
- Unstructured, hard-to-parse log output
- No log levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR)
- No request correlation IDs — impossible to follow a single request across service boundaries
- No centralized log aggregation
- Credentials and sensitive data occasionally leaked in debug logs

In production, this makes debugging incidents slow and unreliable.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Replace `console.*` with `pino` in all Node.js services
```bash
npm install pino pino-pretty
```
- Create `src/logger.js` in each service: `export default pino({ level: process.env.LOG_LEVEL || 'info' })`
- Replace all `console.log` → `logger.info`, `console.error` → `logger.error`, etc.

### 2. Add correlation ID middleware
```js
import { v4 as uuidv4 } from 'uuid';
export function correlationIdMiddleware(req, res, next) {
  req.correlationId = req.headers['x-correlation-id'] || uuidv4();
  res.setHeader('x-correlation-id', req.correlationId);
  req.log = logger.child({ correlationId: req.correlationId, userId: req.user?.id });
  next();
}
```
- Forward `x-correlation-id` header when mcp-server proxies to downstream services

### 3. Log redaction
- Configure pino `redact` option to mask sensitive fields: `['req.headers.authorization', 'body.password', 'apiKey']`

### 4. Add Loki + Grafana log aggregation to docker-compose
```yaml
loki:
  image: grafana/loki:2.9.0
promtail:
  image: grafana/promtail:2.9.0
  volumes:
    - /var/lib/docker/containers:/var/lib/docker/containers:ro
    - ./monitoring/promtail.yml:/etc/promtail/config.yml
```
- Pre-configure a Grafana datasource for Loki
- Create a Grafana log dashboard for each service

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All `console.log`/`console.error` calls are replaced with `pino` logger calls in all three services
- [ ] Every HTTP request log includes `correlationId`, `userId`, `method`, `path`, `statusCode`, `durationMs`
- [ ] The same `correlationId` appears in logs from mcp-server, rass-engine-service, and embedding-service for a single request
- [ ] `Authorization` headers and passwords are redacted from logs
- [ ] Loki datasource is configured in Grafana and log queries work in the development stack
- [ ] Log level is configurable via `LOG_LEVEL` environment variable without code changes

issues.title: [Phase E] Build an evaluation discipline: tracked metrics, baselines, and CI regression gates
issues.body:
## Problem

Phase C introduces evaluation tooling, but without an ongoing discipline, quality metrics will drift. There is no:
- Enforced baseline that prevents quality regressions from being merged
- Dashboard showing quality trends over time
- Clear ownership of the test set (who adds questions? who validates answers?)
- Connection between code changes and quality impact

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Define the evaluation baseline contract
Create `evaluation/BASELINE.json`:
```json
{
  "version": "1.0",
  "measured_at": "2025-01-01T00:00:00Z",
  "git_sha": "abc123",
  "metrics": {
    "context_relevance_mean": 0.78,
    "answer_faithfulness_mean": 0.82,
    "answer_relevance_mean": 0.75,
    "recall_at_5_mean": 0.85,
    "latency_p95_ms": 2100
  },
  "regression_thresholds_pct": {
    "context_relevance": 5,
    "answer_faithfulness": 5,
    "answer_relevance": 5,
    "recall_at_5": 5,
    "latency_p95": 20
  }
}
```

### 2. CI regression gate
- GitHub Actions workflow: on every PR to `main`, run a lightweight eval suite (5 questions) against a local RASS stack
- `evaluation/compare_runs.py --baseline BASELINE.json --current latest_run.json --fail-on-regression`
- PR check fails if any metric regresses beyond the threshold

### 3. Quality trend dashboard
- Store each eval run result in a time-series format
- Create a Grafana panel that plots `context_relevance`, `answer_faithfulness`, and `recall_at_5` over time
- Datasource: a simple SQLite or Prometheus push-gateway for eval metrics

### 4. Test set contribution process
- Document in `evaluation/README.md` how to add new test cases
- Require that new retrieval features are accompanied by at least 2 new test cases in `test_set.json`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `evaluation/BASELINE.json` is committed and reflects real measured metrics
- [ ] The CI regression gate workflow runs on every PR and fails when any metric regresses beyond threshold
- [ ] A Grafana panel shows quality metrics over time (at least 3 historical data points)
- [ ] `evaluation/README.md` documents the test set format and contribution process
- [ ] Adding a new test case to `test_set.json` causes it to be included in the next CI eval run
- [ ] The regression gate correctly fails when a deliberate 10 % quality regression is introduced in a test PR
